lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Is Banquo gone from court?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Ay, madam, but returns again to-night.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Say to the king, I would attend his leisure

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: For a few words.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Madam, I will.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Nought’s had, all’s spent,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Where our desire is got without content:

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: ’Tis safer to be that which we destroy

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Than by destruction dwell in doubtful joy.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: We have scotch’d the snake, not kill’d it:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: She’ll close and be herself, whilst our poor malice

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Remains in danger of her former tooth.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But let the frame of things disjoint, both the worlds suffer,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Ere we will eat our meal in fear and sleep

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: In the affliction of these terrible dreams

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That shake us nightly: better be with the dead,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whom we, to gain our peace, have sent to peace,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Than on the torture of the mind to lie

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: In restless ecstasy. Duncan is in his grave;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: After life’s fitful fever he sleeps well;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Treason has done his worst: nor steel, nor poison,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Malice domestic, foreign levy, nothing,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Can touch him further.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Come on;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Gentle my lord, sleek o’er your rugged looks;

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Be bright and jovial among your guests to-night.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: So shall I, love; and so, I pray, be you:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Let your remembrance apply to Banquo;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Present him eminence, both with eye and tongue:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Unsafe the while, that we

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Must lave our honours in these flattering streams,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And make our faces vizards to our hearts,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Disguising what they are.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: You must leave this.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: O, full of scorpions is my mind, dear wife!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou know’st that Banquo, and his Fleance, lives.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: But in them nature’s copy’s not eterne.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: There’s comfort yet; they are assailable;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Then be thou jocund: ere the bat hath flown

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: His cloister’d flight, ere to black Hecate’s summons

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The shard-borne beetle with his drowsy hums

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hath rung night’s yawning peal, there shall be done

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: A deed of dreadful note.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: What’s to be done?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Be innocent of the knowledge, dearest chuck,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till thou applaud the deed. Come, seeling night,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Scarf up the tender eye of pitiful day;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And with thy bloody and invisible hand

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Cancel and tear to pieces that great bond

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which keeps me pale! Light thickens; and the crow

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Makes wing to the rooky wood:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Good things of day begin to droop and drowse;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whiles night’s black agents to their preys do rouse.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou marvell’st at my words: but hold thee still:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Things bad begun make strong themselves by ill.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: So, prithee, go with me.

stage_directions.text: Enter Lady Macbeth and a Servant.

How now, my lord! why do you keep alone, Of sorriest fancies your companions making, Using those thoughts which should indeed have died With them they think on? Things without all remedy Should be without regard: what’s done is done.

glosses.definition: in troubled happiness — a better condition than she now has
let the whole universe fall apart

glosses.phrase: on the torture of the mind
glosses.definition: on the rack of mental anguish

glosses.phrase: O, full of scorpions is my mind
glosses.definition: Macbeth's image for his poisoned thoughts
the wood where rooks gather for the night; 'rooky' also means misty/dark

glosses.phrase: Things bad begun make strong themselves by ill
glosses.definition: evil deeds can only be defended with more evil — Macbeth's self-doctrine

annotations.title: The marriage in decline
annotations.body:
Lady Macbeth opens the scene alone: 'Naught's had, all's spent, / Where our desire is got without content.' The line is the play's most economical statement of achieved ambition. They have what they wanted. It is nothing.

When Macbeth enters, the asymmetry of their marriage has reversed. In 1.7 she drove him to the deed; here, he has already decided on Banquo's murder and will not tell her. 'Be innocent of the knowledge, dearest chuck, / Till thou applaud the deed.' The endearment — 'chuck' — is sharper than it looks. He is patronizing her, relegating her to the position she forced him out of.

The scene is the hinge of their relationship. After 2.2 they are partners; after 3.2 they are not. He does not consult her again. When she next appears in a major scene (5.1), she is sleepwalking alone, her husband at Dunsinane without her, her confessions heard only by a doctor and a gentlewoman.
sources: Juliet Dusinberre, *Shakespeare and the Nature of Women* (1975)

annotations.title: 'Full of scorpions is my mind'
annotations.body:
Macbeth's image for his insomnia — 'O, full of scorpions is my mind, dear wife!' — is his most visible admission of internal rot. The scorpions are not metaphor for worry; they are presented as an infestation, creatures living in him.

The image prepares the cavern scene (4.1): the Witches' cauldron bubbling with adders, newts, slow-worms. Macbeth's mind has become what the Witches brew. The distinction between the supernatural and the psychological is dissolving. The 'rooky wood' he invokes at scene's end — 'ere to black Hecate's summons / The shard-borne beetle with his drowsy hums / Hath rung night's yawning peal' — is already the wood of the cavern.

The couplet 'Things bad begun make strong themselves by ill' is the doctrine. Evil, once undertaken, requires more evil to defend itself. The play's last three acts are the working out of this sentence.

trials.prompt: Lady Macbeth's opening 'Naught's had, all's spent' reveals:
options: The play's most compact statement about achieved ambition: they have what they wanted, and it is nothing

trials.prompt: Macbeth's decision not to tell Lady Macbeth about Banquo's planned murder means:
options: The asymmetry of their marriage has reversed; after 3.2 they are not partners
She has asked to be excluded

trials.prompt: 'Full of scorpions is my mind' suggests:
options: His mind has become what the Witches brew — supernatural and psychological collapsing together
He has been stung by a scorpion
